title: Q-LEAK: Quantum-Based LEAKage Verification for Side-Channel Countermeasures
authors: Walid El Maouaki, Alberto Marchisio, Muhammad Shafique
abstract: Formal verification of power side-channel leakage and its countermeasures in cryptographic algorithms is challenging, as SAT-based methods fail to scale on XOR-heavy, time-unrolled cryptographic circuits with realistic leakage models. We construct compact Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) cases modeling one-bit leakage under two-trace conditions, linking key dependence and state evolution. Classical solvers quickly reach complexity limits, so we propose Q-LEAK, a quantum-based verification approach using Grover's algorithm, compiling each CNF into an oracle and applying amplitude amplification to search in O(sqrt(N)) oracle calls, with oracles that encode the two-trace leakage predicate and the CNF constraints. Benchmarking against classical SAT shows both potential gains and practical resource limits. In noiseless tests on 5-7 variable benchmarks, Q-LEAK consistently recovered a satisfying assignment within 1-4 tries, with marked bitstrings amplified clearly above the background distribution, exceeding 20 percent probability. The evaluation of Q-LEAK on real quantum hardware revealed at least one classically verified SAT assignment, despite the presence of noise. These results point to a potential path toward quantum-assisted verification of side-channel protections.
summary: This paper introduces Q-LEAK, a quantum algorithm designed to accelerate the formal verification of cryptographic side-channel countermeasures. By mapping leakage vulnerabilities to a Boolean satisfiability problem, the authors demonstrate that Grover-based search can provide a quadratic speedup over classical solvers. The approach was validated through both large-scale simulations and experiments on real IBM quantum hardware.
main_problem: The computational difficulty of formally verifying power side-channel leakage countermeasures in cryptographic algorithms due to the exponential scaling of classical SAT solvers.
main_result: The proposed Q-LEAK framework achieves a quadratic speedup using Grover's algorithm and successfully recovers leakage-inducing assignments in both noiseless simulations and on real IBM quantum hardware.
method: The authors encode side-channel leakage models into Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) and implement a quantum oracle using Grover's algorithm and the BBHT variant for unstructured search.
model_or_system: The framework models cryptographic hardware implementations (like AES) using a one-bit leakage model under two-trace conditions, implemented via a reversible Grover oracle.
key_observables: Probability distributions of bitstrings (peaks for SAT, flat for UNSAT), Pearson chi-squared goodness-of-fit, and Hamming weight/distance leakage patterns.
important_parameters: Number of variables (n), clause density (rho), number of satisfying assignments (K), and the classical break-even exponent (alpha_min).
main_assumptions: The study assumes a 1-bit leakage model and limited circuit unrolling (T=1); it also notes that current scalability is limited by oracle ancilla requirements and gate depth.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the verification pipeline; Figure 2 compares classical vs. quantum scaling; Figure 3/4 details the Q-LEAK framework and oracle construction; Figure 5 shows circuit sketches; Figure 6/7 displays probability distributions and hardware histograms; Figure 8 shows the classical break-even threshold.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the side-channel verification problem, defines the Q-LEAK algorithmic framework and oracle construction, provides a theoretical complexity analysis, presents simulation and hardware validation results, and concludes with scalability projections and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Cryptographic algorithm is implemented as a cipher in hardware, exposed to power side-channel attacks. Masking countermeasures are applied to protect intermediate values, and formal verification ensures that the final implementation remains leakage-free after synthesis.

Fig. 2. Asymptotic scaling of search cost versus the number of variables n on a log scale: classical worst-case scaling ∼2n (red) versus Q-LEAK with density-aware oracle cost ∼ρn · 2n/2 (green), where ρ = m/n is the clause density. For ρ = 20, the crossover occurs near n⋆≈16.8, with QLEAK showing a widening advantage for larger n.
Fig. 3. Overview of the main contributions of Q-LEAK. Green boxes highlight the proposed components.

Fig. 4. Q-LEAK framework. Compile the protected cipher and BIT model into a two-trace, T-cycle CNF, map it to an oracle UF ; then run Grover+BBHT over the variable qubits. Peaks indicate SAT (leak), near-uniform spectra indicate UNSAT (secure at the chosen model and T).

Fig. 4 shows the Q-LEAK flow. Starting from a protected cipher and the given BIT power-leakage model, (1) we encode logic and leakage into a CNF over key, data, randomness, and internal wires. (2) The design is duplicated into two traces and unrolled for T cycles; we tie plaintexts (P = P ′), set key constraints, and impose the chosen leakage relation to ask: Does leakage reveal the key? (3) This CNF is compiled into a reversible oracle UF and queried with the Grover/BBHT loop, then measurements return candidate assignments.
